chunks.text: Professor Bob Brayton, pioneer in logic synthesis and formal verification, has died - EECS at Berkeley EECS Professor Emeritus Robert K. Brayton has passed away. He was 91. A trained electrical engineer and mathematician, Bob Brayton was a pioneer in logic synthesis and formal verification. His career spanned multiple decades, during which he made groundbreaking contributions to the field of electrical engineering. For the first 26 years of his career, he was a member of the Mathematical Sciences Department at IBM’s T.J. Watson Research Center, where he led the Yorktown Silicon Compiler team. His work there helped create one of the most advanced logic synthesis systems of its time. Additionally, Bob co-developed the sparse tableau methodology, which remains a foundational technique in modern circuit simulation. In 1987, Brayton joined the EECS faculty at UC Berkeley, where he would go on to build long-standing collaborations with many colleagues, including EECS Professor Alberto Sangiovanni-Vincentelli. Together, they authored numerous influential papers and books. Their collaborative work on the Multi-level Logic Synthesis System was widely used in the design and verification of complex logic systems, significantly impacting the development of Electronic Design Automation (EDA). Furthermore, Brayton’s contributions to logic synthesis led to the development of practical techniques such as retiming and optimization of circuits for high speed. Professor Brayton’s legacy is celebrated through the many students, colleagues, and researchers he mentored and inspired. His contributions to the advancement of logic synthesis and circuit design continue to shape the field today. In his honor, Professor

Professor Brayton’s legacy is celebrated through the many students, colleagues, and researchers he mentored and inspired. His contributions to the advancement of logic synthesis and circuit design continue to shape the field today. In his honor, Professor Brayton’s former students have raised close to $1 million to name a prominent space in the new Engineering center: The Robert K. and Ruth B. Brayton Community Room.
